content:
Python 3.13 shipped free-threaded mode. Your thread pool is still doing the work of one core.

4 min read
Just now

--

I ran the same CPU-bound worker on Python 3.13 free-threaded mode and Python 3.12. Eight threads. Real workload. The 3.13 result was 4.4 seconds. The 3.12 result was 4.1 seconds.
The GIL was supposed to be the bottleneck. I removed it. Things got slower.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That moment broke something in my mental model — and fixing it changed how I write concurrent Python entirely.
The Profiler Did Not Lie
Here is what I was expecting: remove the Global Interpreter Lock, add threads, watch all eight cores spike to 100%, enjoy the speedup.
Here is what actually happened. One core hit 90%. The other seven hovered around 12 to 15%, spiking occasionally, never sustaining. The profiler showed the threads running — technically. But the work was not distributing the way I assumed it would.
The GIL was not the only thing serializing my code. It was just the most famous thing.
What the GIL Actually Protected (And What You Thought It Did)
